Go to ACL ARR 2021 November homepage

Hyperlink-induced Pre-training for Passage Retrieval of Open-domain Question Answering

Blind Submission by November • Hyperlink-induced Pre-training for Passage Retrieval of Open-domain Question Answering

Anonymous
17 Nov 2021 (modified: 06 May 2023)ACL ARR 2021 November Blind SubmissionReaders: EveryoneShow Revisions
Abstract: To alleviate the data scarcity problem in training question answering systems, recent works propose additional intermediate pre-training for dense passage retrieval (DPR). However, there still remains a large discrepancy between the provided upstream signals and the downstream question-passage relevance, which leads to less improvement. To bridge this gap, we propose the Hyperlink-induced Pre-training (HLP), a method to pre-train the dense retriever with the text relevance induced by hyperlink-based topology within Web documents. We demonstrate that the hyperlink-based structures of dual-link and co-mention can provide effective relevance signals for large-scale pre-training that better facilitate downstream passage retrieval. We investigate the effectiveness of our approach across a wide range of open-domain QA datasets under zero-shot, few-shot, multi-hop, and out-of-domain scenarios. The experiments show our HLP outperforms the BM25 by up to 7 points as well as other pre-training methods by up to 30 points in terms of top-20 retrieval accuracy under the zero-shot scenario. Furthermore, HLP significantly outperforms other pre-training methods under the other scenarios.
Revealed to Jiawei Zhou, Xiaoguang Li, Lifeng Shang, Lan Luo, Ke Zhan, Enrui Hu, Xinyu Zhang, Hao Jiang, Zhao Cao, Fan Yu, Xin Jiang, Qun Liu, Lei Chen
15 Nov 2021 (modified: 06 May 2023)ACL ARR 2021 November Submission
Authors:Jiawei Zhou, Xiaoguang Li, Lifeng Shang, Lan Luo, Ke Zhan, Enrui Hu, Xinyu Zhang, Hao Jiang, Zhao Cao, Fan Yu, Xin Jiang, Qun Liu, Lei Chen
TL;DR: we propose Hyperlink-induced Pre-training (HLP), a pre-training method to learn question-passage relevance induced by hyperlink-based structures within Web documents for OpenQA passage retrieval.

Meta Review of Paper894 by Area Chair Z8ZN

Meta Review of Paper894 by Area Chair Z8ZN

ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Area Chair Z8ZN
29 Dec 2021 (modified: 29 Dec 2021)ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Meta ReviewReaders: Paper894 Senior Area Chairs, Paper894 Area Chairs, Paper894 Authors, Paper894 Reviewers, Program ChairsShow Revisions
Metareview:

The authors propose a data-augmentation technique for pre-training neural passage retrieval models: use Wikipedia hyperlinks to generate query/passage pairs. For example, if two paragraphs from two different Wikipedia pages have direct hyperlinks to each other, that pair of paragraphs can be used as examples for pre-training the neural passage retrieval model.

Summary Of Reasons To Publish:
  • The authors addressed most of the issues raised in the previous submission
  • The proposed data augmentation technique is novel, simple, and powerful.
  • Models pre-trained with data from the proposed technique advance the state-of-the-art across a number of question answering benchmarks, especially in the zero-shot setting.
  • Analyses including ablations and case studies enhance the evaluation.
  • Source code and pre-training data will be made public.
Summary Of Suggested Revisions:
  • There are still some issues with notation. Reviewer W4oM makes some specific suggestions, and reviewer isrn suggests bringing the notation from Appendix E to the main text.
  • It would be helpful to address the interaction of negative passages and batch size (as noted by reviewer isrn)
  • It would be nice to see performance on the BEIR benchmark.

Reviewers W4oM and isrn also provide some suggested wording improvements

Overall Assessment: 4 = There are minor points that may be revised
Suggested Venues:

This would be a good fit for the top-tier *ACL conferences, especially if they're looking for passage retrieval submissions.

[–][+]

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer VLVf

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer VLVf

ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Reviewer VLVf
27 Dec 2021ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Official ReviewReaders: Program Chairs, Paper894 Senior Area Chairs, Paper894 Area Chairs, Paper894 Reviewers, Paper894 AuthorsShow Revisions
Paper Summary:

This paper proposes a method to improve the performance of the retriever typically used in the recent open-domain question answering systems. It uses the hyperlink structure in the Web documents to create pseudo question-passage pairs to train the retriever. The paper tests two approaches: (1) dual-link that extracts the question-passage pairs from two pages that link to each other, and (2) co-mention that extracts the pairs from two pages that link to a same page. It adopts a standard bi-encoder trained using the extracted question-passage pairs. Experiments are conducted using five standard question answering datasets under zero-shot, few-shot, multi-hop, and out-of-domain scenarios, and report enhanced empirical performance.

Summary Of Strengths:
  • Dataset creation method that is highly useful to train the retriever for open domain question answering
  • Enhanced retrieval performance in popular question answering benchmarks
  • Extensive experiments that validate the effectiveness of the proposed method
  • Well-written paper
Summary Of Weaknesses:

The method significantly underperform BM25 in the MS MARCO dataset, which is one of the most popular retrieval benchmark. This suggests that the applicability of the proposed method to general QA domain is limited.

Comments, Suggestions And Typos:

N/A

Overall Assessment: 3.5
Confidence: 4 = Quite sure. I tried to check the important points carefully. It's unlikely, though conceivable, that I missed something that should affect my ratings.
Best Paper: No
Replicability: 5 = They could easily reproduce the results.
Datasets: 4 = Useful: I would recommend the new datasets to other researchers or developers for their ongoing work.
Software: 4 = Useful: I would recommend the new software to other researchers or developers for their ongoing work.
Author Identity Guess: 1 = I do not have even an educated guess about author identity.
[–][+]

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer xJ9T

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer xJ9T

ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Reviewer xJ9T
26 Dec 2021ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Official ReviewReaders: Program Chairs, Paper894 Senior Area Chairs, Paper894 Area Chairs, Paper894 Reviewers, Paper894 AuthorsShow Revisions
Paper Summary:

This paper addresses the Passage Retrieval of Open-domain Question Answering by Hyperlink-induced Supervision (HIS). These authors present a hyperlink-induced relevance construction methodology, Dual-link and Co-mention. and then they develop hyperlink-based pre-training task, HIS, to learn text relevance, which automatically extracts relevant documents as Q-P pairs guaranteed by the hyperlink-based Web topology. The results of investigation suggest that HIS can help to detect answers for open-domain question answering tasks and other scenarios, few-shot Learning and multi-hop Retrieval task.

Summary Of Strengths:
  1. This paper is well written.
  2. The authors have answered all issued raised by the reviewers.
Summary Of Weaknesses:

The authors have answered all issued raised by the reviewers. I have no further comments.

Comments, Suggestions And Typos:

The authors have answered all issued raised by the reviewers. I have no further comments.

Overall Assessment: 4 = Strong: This paper is of significant interest (for broad or narrow sub-communities), and warrants acceptance in a top-tier *ACL venue if space allows.
Confidence: 3 = Pretty sure, but there's a chance I missed something. Although I have a good feel for this area in general, I did not carefully check the paper's details, e.g., the math or experimental design.
Best Paper: No
Replicability: 4 = They could mostly reproduce the results, but there may be some variation because of sample variance or minor variations in their interpretation of the protocol or method.
Datasets: 3 = Potentially useful: Someone might find the new datasets useful for their work.
Software: 4 = Useful: I would recommend the new software to other researchers or developers for their ongoing work.
Author Identity Guess: 1 = I do not have even an educated guess about author identity.
[–][+]

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer W4oM

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer W4oM

ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Reviewer W4oM
26 Dec 2021 (modified: 26 Dec 2021)ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Official ReviewReaders: Program Chairs, Paper894 Senior Area Chairs, Paper894 Area Chairs, Paper894 Reviewers, Paper894 AuthorsShow Revisions
Paper Summary:

This paper proposes to exploit the hyperlink structure of web documents to create synthetic pre-training examples for neural IR. With pre-training examples extracted using two specific hyperlink patterns, namely dual-link and co-mention, the authors show that the performance of neural IR can be improved in various test settings: in-domain, out-of-domain, few-shot, zero-shot and multi-hop. The proposed method also outperforms a number of existing synthetic pre-training baselines

Summary Of Strengths:
  1. The paper proposes a simple yet powerful data augmentation method for neural IR.
  2. The evaluation is quite extensive, with strong results.
  3. Source code and pre-training data will be made public.
Summary Of Weaknesses:

While the authors have been partially successful in improving the mathematical notation of the paper (for example, equations 4 and 6 are now factually more accurate), I still find it imprecise/non-uniform and hard to follow in a number of places (mainly due to vaguely defined variables and/or disparity between their definitions and references). Please see the Comments section for some examples. I am recommending a stronger accept this time, but I still strongly encourage the authors to further work on the notation, being extra careful about the preciseness of variable definitions and their compatibility with their use.

Comments, Suggestions And Typos:
  1. On mathematical notation: Let us consider “topical entity e_P” in line 189 as an example. The variable e_* was previously defined as the “topical entity” of a document (line 180), but is applied in line 189 to a passage without any further clarification. The authors are probably referring to the “topical entity” of the document containing passage p, but this more general usage of the variable e_* should be covered by its definition, otherwise readers are left with having to guess. The uppercasing of P in e_P also adds to the confusion. The term “topical entity” is itself somewhat vague to start with in line 180. A similar case of non-uniform notation involves the variable t_Q in line 207, because Q (or should it be q?) is not a document.
  2. I appreciate the authors’ response to my original question about whether the title of a page should be considered the answer to a pseudo-question in the situation described in lines 206–217 (in the current version of the paper). While I agree with the response (which is not exactly misaligned with my original understanding), my suggestion was mostly about whether to use the word “answer”, which might be too strong since the “question” sentence may or may not be even asking an actual question. A more relaxed term such as “a target entity” might be more appropriate.
  3. The title of the paper might need a minor modification: "Hyperlink-induced Pre-training for Passage Retrieval in Open-domain Question Answering"?
Overall Assessment: 4 = Strong: This paper is of significant interest (for broad or narrow sub-communities), and warrants acceptance in a top-tier *ACL venue if space allows.
Confidence: 4 = Quite sure. I tried to check the important points carefully. It's unlikely, though conceivable, that I missed something that should affect my ratings.
Best Paper: No
Replicability: 4 = They could mostly reproduce the results, but there may be some variation because of sample variance or minor variations in their interpretation of the protocol or method.
Datasets: 3 = Potentially useful: Someone might find the new datasets useful for their work.
Software: 3 = Potentially useful: Someone might find the new software useful for their work.
Author Identity Guess: 1 = I do not have even an educated guess about author identity.
[–][+]

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer isrn

Official Review of Paper894 by Reviewer isrn

ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Reviewer isrn
11 Dec 2021ACL ARR 2021 November Paper894 Official ReviewReaders: Program Chairs, Paper894 Senior Area Chairs, Paper894 Area Chairs, Paper894 Reviewers, Paper894 AuthorsShow Revisions
Paper Summary:

The paper proposes a new pre-training task for dense retrieval, based on the hyperlink topology of Wikipedia passages. Training on this dataset is shown to produce better retrieval results compared to previous methods, such as ICT, BFS and WLP, especially in the zero-shot / few-shot setting.

Summary Of Strengths:

The paper has multiple strengths, sufficient for publication in an *ACL venue, given the weaknesses are addressed:

  • The method of creating pre-training data is nicely motivated by the definition of relevance as "evidence existence" and "answer relevance".
  • Good coverage of recent relevant work
  • Baseline implementations are strong
  • Results are very strong, especially in the zero-shot setting.
  • Sufficient analysis and ablations
Summary Of Weaknesses:
  • The description of HLP in section 3 is pretty confusing (even with figure 2). The easiest to understand for me was Appendix E, with the notation. I would bring this into the main text (doesn't have to be in algorithm form, but use the notation to lay out the definitions).
  • BEIR benchmark is becoming standard for zero-shot retrieval, I think this would be a better choice for the zero-shot comparison, than just BioASQ and MSMARCO, and would allow future work to more easily compare to this paper.
  • Negative passages: there is no description of what these are except for the appendix. And there, it surprisingly turns out to be random negatives. In-batch negatives are also random negatives, so essentially this is similar to not using random negatives. Most likely, the improvement is from bigger effective batch size. It is worth clarifying this detail in the text (with additional experiments if needed)
  • Lines 400-401: I don't really buy the explanation here, as the overlap numbers are pretty close, but the NQ vs/ Trivia zero-shot numbers are very different (compared to BM25). I would either take this out, or provide more evidence.
  • Table 1: MSS row is absent in w/ fine-tuning section. Any reason for this?
  • It would be great if you could release the pre-training dataset and models for replicability
Comments, Suggestions And Typos:

Typos / grammar:

  • line 51: "easily achieving" ?
  • line 150: datasaset
  • line 203: "triple that induced by"

Minor: why do you use a different Wikipedia snapshot for pre-training than the retrieval index?

Overall Assessment: 4 = Strong: This paper is of significant interest (for broad or narrow sub-communities), and warrants acceptance in a top-tier *ACL venue if space allows.
Confidence: 5 = Positive that my evaluation is correct. I read the paper very carefully and am familiar with related work.
Best Paper: No
Replicability: 4 = They could mostly reproduce the results, but there may be some variation because of sample variance or minor variations in their interpretation of the protocol or method.
Datasets: 1 = No usable datasets submitted.
Software: 1 = No usable software released.
Author Identity Guess: 1 = I do not have even an educated guess about author identity.
